cards.question: Fabric: A continuous piece of cloth generally used as dress material, is called fabric.
cards.text_html: Fabric: A continuous piece of cloth generally used as dress material, is called fabric.

cards.question: Wool: It is obtained from the fleece of domestic goats, sheep, rabbits, etc. The colour of wool fibres may vary from of f-white to light cream. Fabrics made from wool are soft, smooth, absorbent, and do not wrinkle easily.
cards.text_html: Wool: It is obtained from the fleece of domestic goats, sheep, rabbits, etc. The colour of wool fibres may vary from of f-white to light cream. Fabrics made from wool are soft, smooth, absorbent, and do not wrinkle easily.

cards.question: Silk: Silk is a natural, protein filament produced by silk worms. Fabrics made from silk are soft, fine, smooth, lustrous, warm, and stronger than wool. It is called the ‘ Queen of the Fibres ’and is used for formal wear.
cards.text_html: Silk: Silk is a natural, protein filament produced by silk worms. Fabrics made from silk are soft, fine, smooth, lustrous, warm, and stronger than wool. It is called the ‘Queen of the Fibres’and is used for formal wear.

cards.question: Rayon: It is a man-made filament fibre that is lustrous, smooth, cool, and absorbent but is weak in nature. It wrinkles very easily. Because of its close resemblance to silk, rayon is also called ‘artificial silk’ or ‘art silk.’
cards.text_html: Rayon: It is a man-made filament fibre that is lustrous, smooth, cool, and absorbent but is weak in nature. It wrinkles very easily. Because of its close resemblance to silk, rayon is also called ‘artificial silk’ or ‘art silk.’

cards.question: Synthetic fibres: Synthetic fibres are made from petroleum products. Nylon, polyester, acrylic, etc., are examples of synthetic fibres. Synthetics do not wrinkle and can be made dull or shiny. They have good strength and are easy to wash and dry quickly.
cards.text_html: Synthetic fibres: Synthetic fibres are made from petroleum products. Nylon, polyester, acrylic, etc., are examples of synthetic fibres. Synthetics do not wrinkle and can be made dull or shiny. They have good strength and are easy to wash and dry quickly.

cards.question: Fibres: All cloth materials are made up of long, narrow, thin structures called fibres. Fibres are obtained from natural as well as man-made sources.
cards.text_html: Fibres: All cloth materials are made up of long, narrow, thin structures called fibres. Fibres are obtained from natural as well as man-made sources.

cards.question: Fabrics are made from yarn. Yarn is made from fibres.
cards.text_html:
• Fabrics are made from yarn.
  • Yarn is made from fibres.

cards.question: Natural Sources: Cotton, jute, silk, wool, etc., are obtained from natural sources- plants or animals.
cards.text_html: Natural Sources: Cotton, jute, silk, wool, etc., are obtained from natural sources- plants or animals.

cards.question: Man-made Sources: Polyester, nylon, rayon etc., are man-made materials used for making clothes.
cards.text_html: Man-made Sources: Polyester, nylon, rayon etc., are man-made materials used for making clothes.

cards.question: Natural fibres: it can be divided into plant fibres animal fibres
cards.text_html:
Natural fibres: it can be divided into

  1. plant fibres
  2. animal fibres

cards.question: Plant fibres: The fibres which are found on some part/parts of the plant are called plant fibres. Cotton has fibres on its seed. Coconut has fibres on its fruit. The banana tree has fibres on its leaves. Flax and jute have fibres on their stem.
cards.text_html:
Plant fibres: The fibres which are found on some part/parts of the plant are called plant fibres.

  1. Cotton has fibres on its seed.
  2. Coconut has fibres on its fruit.
  3. The banana tree has fibres on its leaves.
  4. Flax and jute have fibres on their stem.

cards.question: Animal fibres: The fibres which are found on the skin of animals or the cocoons of beetles are called animal fibres. Most of the wool is obtained from the fleece of the sheep. Some amount of wool is obtained from the hair of goats, camels, and yak. Silk is obtained from the cocoon. The pupa of a silkworm spins silk around itself.
cards.text_html:
Animal fibres: The fibres which are found on the skin of animals or the cocoons of beetles are called animal fibres.

  1. Most of the wool is obtained from the fleece of the sheep.
  2. Some amount of wool is obtained from the hair of goats, camels, and yak.
  3. Silk is obtained from the cocoon. The pupa of a silkworm spins silk around itself.

cards.question: Synthetic fibres or Artificial fibres: The fibres obtained from substances that are not obtained from plant or animal sources are called synthetic fibres or artificial fibres.
cards.text_html: Synthetic fibres or Artificial fibres: The fibres obtained from substances that are not obtained from plant or animal sources are called synthetic fibres or artificial fibres.

cards.question: Synthetic fibres Synthetic fibres are more durable than natural fibres. Synthetic fibres do not absorb. Synthetic fibres are very uncomfortable in hot and humid climates. Nylon, terylene, polyester, etc, are example of artificial fibres.
cards.text_html:
Synthetic fibres

  • Synthetic fibres are more durable than natural fibres.
  • Synthetic fibres do not absorb.
  • Synthetic fibres are very uncomfortable in hot and humid climates.
  • Nylon, terylene, polyester, etc, are example of artificial fibres.

cards.question: Fibers are two types – Natural and Man-made. Natural fibers - Natural fibers are obtained from plants and animals; such as jute, cotton, wool, silk, etc. Manmade fibers - Fibers that are synthesized in the laboratory are called manmade fibers, such as terylene, terry-cotton, acrylic, etc.
cards.text_html:
Fibers are two types – Natural and Man-made.
Natural fibers- Natural fibers are obtained from plants and animals; such as jute, cotton, wool, silk, etc.
Manmade fibers- Fibers that are synthesized in the laboratory are called manmade fibers, such as terylene, terry-cotton, acrylic, etc.

cards.question: Natural fiber: Natural fibers can be classified into two types – Plant fiber and Animal fiber. Plant Fiber: Fiber obtained from plants is called plant fiber. For example - cotton, jute, flex, etc. Animal Fiber: Fiber obtained from animals is called animal fiber. For example - wool and silk.
cards.text_html:
Natural fiber: Natural fibers can be classified into two types – Plant fiber and Animal fiber.
Plant Fiber: Fiber obtained from plants is called plant fiber. For example - cotton, jute, flex, etc.
Animal Fiber: Fiber obtained from animals is called animal fiber. For example - wool and silk.

cards.question: Production of cotton: Cotton is grown in Maharashtra, Gujarat, Punjab, Rajasthan, Tamil Nadu, and Madhya Pradesh.
cards.text_html: Production of cotton: Cotton is grown in Maharashtra, Gujarat, Punjab, Rajasthan, Tamil Nadu, and Madhya Pradesh.

cards.question: Climate required for cotton: Cotton plants need a warm climate. Cotton is planted early in the spring. Black soil is excellent for cotton cultivation.
cards.text_html:
Climate required for cotton:
  • Cotton plants need a warm climate.
  • Cotton is planted early in the spring.
  • Black soil is excellent for cotton cultivation.

cards.question: Cotton bolls: Fruits of cotton plants are spherical-shaped structures of the size of wall nuts which are called cotton bolls. On maturation, cotton bolls burst open, exposing the white fibres.
cards.text_html: Cotton bolls: Fruits of cotton plants are spherical-shaped structures of the size of wall nuts which are called cotton bolls. On maturation, cotton bolls burst open, exposing the white fibres.

cards.question: Cotton fibres: Cotton fibres are obtained from cotton bolls.
cards.text_html: Cotton fibres: Cotton fibres are obtained from cotton bolls.

cards.question: Ginning: The process in which seeds from cotton are pulled out by steel combs is called ginning.
cards.text_html: Ginning: The process in which seeds from cotton are pulled out by steel combs is called ginning.

cards.question: Charkha: Charkha is a machine on which yarn was spun directly from ginned cotton in the olden days.
cards.text_html: Charkha: Charkha is a machine on which yarn was spun directly from ginned cotton in the olden days.

cards.question: Bales: Ginned cotton is compressed tightly into bundles weighing approximately 200 kg called bales.
cards.text_html: Bales: Ginned cotton is compressed tightly into bundles weighing approximately 200 kg called bales.

cards.question: Sliver: Raw cotton from bales is cleaned, combed and straightened, and finally converted into rope-like strands called sliver . A sliver of cotton is a loose strand or rope of cotton fibres.
cards.text_html: Sliver: Raw cotton from bales is cleaned, combed and straightened, and finally converted into rope-like strands called sliver. A sliver of cotton is a loose strand or rope of cotton fibres.

cards.question: There are three varieties of cotton. Long staple cotton: It has the largest fibre length which varies between 24 mm to 27 mm. It is commonly called Egyptian cotton and is the finest variety. It is grown in the states of Punjab, Haryana, Maharashtra, Tamil Nadu, and Andhra Pradesh. Medium staple cotton: Its fibre length is between 20 mm to 24 mm. It has grown in the states of Punjab, Rajasthan, and Tamil Nadu. Short staple cotton: Its fibre length is less than 19 mm. it is grown in the states Uttar Pradesh, Andhra Pradesh, Rajasthan, Haryana, and Punjab.
cards.text_html:
There are three varieties of cotton.

  1. Long staple cotton: It has the largest fibre length which varies between 24 mm to 27 mm. It is commonly called Egyptian cotton and is the finest variety. It is grown in the states of Punjab, Haryana, Maharashtra, Tamil Nadu, and Andhra Pradesh.
  2. Medium staple cotton: Its fibre length is between 20 mm to 24 mm. It has grown in the states of Punjab, Rajasthan, and Tamil Nadu.
  3. Short staple cotton: Its fibre length is less than 19 mm. it is grown in the states Uttar Pradesh, Andhra Pradesh, Rajasthan, Haryana, and Punjab.

cards.question: Cotton seed uses The cotton seed oil is used in the manufacture of vanaspati ghee. The residue left after the extraction of the cotton seed oil is called cotton seed cake. Cotton seed cake is used as animal feed.
cards.text_html:
Cotton seed uses

  • The cotton seed oil is used in the manufacture of vanaspati ghee.
  • The residue left after the extraction of the cotton seed oil is called cotton seed cake. Cotton seed cake is used as animal feed.

cards.question: Jute: Jute is a plant fibre obtained from the stem of a plant called Datsun. In India, jute is mainly grown in west Bengal, Assam, and Bihar, in then law-lying areas around the rivers Ganga and Brahmaputra.
cards.text_html:
Jute: Jute is a plant fibre obtained from the stem of a plant called Datsun.
In India, jute is mainly grown in west Bengal, Assam, and Bihar, in then law-lying areas around the rivers Ganga and Brahmaputra.

cards.question: Cultivation of Jute: Its seeds are sown in March or April or during the rainy season. Jute plant grows 6 to 12 feet in height. The jute bears a yellow flower. They just cut the flowering stage. It is because a good quantity of jute is obtained from its stems only at this stage. After harvesting the stalks of the plant are tied into bundles and left in stagnant ponds water for about 20 days. The stems rot and fibres are separated by hands. The fibres do obtain are dried in the sun and tied into small bundles. They are then spun and woven into a textile.
cards.text_html:
Cultivation of Jute: 
  • Its seeds are sown in March or April or during the rainy season. Jute plant grows 6 to 12 feet in height.
  • The jute bears a yellow flower. They just cut the flowering stage. It is because a good quantity of jute is obtained from its stems only at this stage.
  • After harvesting the stalks of the plant are tied into bundles and left in stagnant ponds water for about 20 days.
  • The stems rot and fibres are separated by hands.
  • The fibres do obtain are dried in the sun and tied into small bundles. They are then spun and woven into a textile.

cards.question: Uses of Jute: Jute is the most important natural fiber after cotton. It is very affordable to make cloths, sacks, carpets, bags, ropes, yarn, and coasters. The cloth that is made from jute can use to wrap cotton bales as well as any construction protector. It is also used for bedding foundation, boot and shoe linings, and tailors bag packing.
cards.text_html:
Uses of Jute:
  • Jute is the most important natural fiber after cotton. It is very affordable to make cloths, sacks, carpets, bags, ropes, yarn, and coasters.
  • The cloth that is made from jute can use to wrap cotton bales as well as any construction protector.
  • It is also used for bedding foundation, boot and shoe linings, and tailors bag packing.

cards.question: Uses of coconut fibre: The fibre obtained from the outer covering of the coconut is called coir. The coir is used for making foot mats, floor carpets, ropes, and for filling mattresses and pillows.
cards.text_html:
Uses of coconut fibre:
  • The fibre obtained from the outer covering of the coconut is called coir.
  • The coir is used for making foot mats, floor carpets, ropes, and for filling mattresses and pillows.

cards.question: Spinning: The process of making yarn from fibre is called spinning. Spinning is the process of changing fibre. Raw cotton is supplied to the industry in the form of bales.
cards.text_html:
Spinning:

  • The process of making yarn from fibre is called spinning.
  • Spinning is the process of changing fibre. Raw cotton is supplied to the industry in the form of bales.

cards.question: Spinning draws out the short fibres from the cotton wool and twists them together into a long continuous thread called yarn.
cards.text_html: Spinning draws out the short fibres from the cotton wool and twists them together into a long continuous thread called yarn.

cards.question: Steps in making cotton yarn The raw cotton is loosened and cleaned; to remove the straw and dried leaves. The cleaned cotton is then fed into a machine. The cotton fibre is combed, straightened, and converted into a rope-like structure called sliver. The sliver of cotton fibre is converted into yarn by spinning using machines.
cards.text_html:
Steps in making cotton yarn
  • The raw cotton is loosened and cleaned; to remove the straw and dried leaves.
  • The cleaned cotton is then fed into a machine. The cotton fibre is combed, straightened, and converted into a rope-like structure called sliver.
  • The sliver of cotton fibre is converted into yarn by spinning using machines.

cards.question: A simple device used for spinning is a head spindle or the takli. Another hand-operated device for spinning is the charkha.
cards.text_html: A simple device used for spinning is a head spindle or the takli. Another hand-operated device for spinning is the charkha.

cards.question: ​​​​​​​ Knitting: Knitting is a method by which a single thread or yarn may be turned into a cloth with the help of hands or a machine.
cards.text_html: ​​​​​​​Knitting: Knitting is a method by which a single thread or yarn may be turned into a cloth with the help of hands or a machine.

cards.question: Weaving: The process of arranging two sets of yarns together to make a fabric with the help of a handloom or power loom is called weaving.
cards.text_html: Weaving: The process of arranging two sets of yarns together to make a fabric with the help of a handloom or power loom is called weaving.

cards.question: ​​​​​​​ Weaving: The process of weaving is done on a loom. Weaving is done manually on handlooms. Weavers weaving on a small scale use handlooms. On large scale, cotton fabrics are made by big machines that run on electricity. These machines are called power looms.
cards.text_html:
​​​​​​​Weaving: 

  • The process of weaving is done on a loom. Weaving is done manually on handlooms.
  • Weavers weaving on a small scale use handlooms.
  • On large scale, cotton fabrics are made by big machines that run on electricity. These machines are called power looms.

cards.question: In any kind of loom, the yarn placed lengthwise on the frames is called warp. Another yarn is wound on a wooden reel and is placed in the shuttle of the machine. The machine carries the thread in the shuttle back and forth across the wrap.
cards.text_html:
• In any kind of loom, the yarn placed lengthwise on the frames is called warp.
  • Another yarn is wound on a wooden reel and is placed in the shuttle of the machine. The machine carries the thread in the shuttle back and forth across the wrap.

cards.question: The yarn which the shuttle spreads across the warp is called weft. During weaving the weft threads cross over under the warp threads ultimately and make the fabric.
cards.text_html:
• The yarn which the shuttle spreads across the warp is called weft.
  • During weaving the weft threads cross over under the warp threads ultimately and make the fabric.

cards.question: In ancient Egypt, cotton and flax plants were cultivated near the river Nile and used for marking fabrics.
cards.text_html: In ancient Egypt, cotton and flax plants were cultivated near the river Nile and used for marking fabrics.

cards.question: In China, silkworms were domesticated to produce silk fibres. The fibres were then spun into a thread, which was woven to make silk cloth.
cards.text_html:
• In China, silkworms were domesticated to produce silk fibres.
  • The fibres were then spun into a thread, which was woven to make silk cloth.

cards.question: People started wearing stitched clothes after the invention of needle about 40,000 to 50,000 years ago.
cards.text_html: People started wearing stitched clothes after the invention of needle about 40,000 to 50,000 years ago.

cards.question: Saree, dhoti, lungi and turban are unstitched pieces of fabric. Jeans, suits, shirts and trousers are stitched clothes.
cards.text_html:
Saree, dhoti, lungi and turban are unstitched pieces of fabric.
Jeans, suits, shirts and trousers are stitched clothes.

cards.question: Following are reasons for wearing clothes: Clothes protect us from the cold winds, heat, and other climatic conditions. Clothes protect us from minor injuries. Clothes help us to maintain our body at a suitable temperature.
cards.text_html:
Following are reasons for wearing clothes:
  1. Clothes protect us from the cold winds, heat, and other climatic conditions.
  2. Clothes protect us from minor injuries.
  3. Clothes help us to maintain our body at a suitable temperature.
